questions.q: What is CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense, Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance) and how does it differ from CSMA/CD used in wired Ethernet?
options: CSMA/CA senses the medium before transmitting and uses exponential backoff and RTS/CTS handshakes to avoid collisions (half-duplex); CSMA/CD detects collisions after sending and retransmits (full-duplex wired), which is impractical for wireless where collision detection is difficult
CSMA/CA and CSMA/CD are identical protocols with different names
CSMA/CD is faster because it does not waste time sensing the medium before transmission
Wireless uses CSMA/CD; wired networks use CSMA/CA
questions.explanation: Wireless cannot detect collisions while transmitting (collision detection requires simultaneous transmit/receive); CSMA/CA prevents collisions via RTS (Request to Send), CTS (Clear to Send), and NAV (Network Allocation Vector). Wired Ethernet (802.3) uses CSMA/CD: sense, transmit, detect collision, retransmit. CSMA/CA is fundamentally different due to half-duplex wireless.

questions.q: What is the hidden terminal problem in wireless networks and how does it occur?
options: A station outside the transmission range of another station cannot sense its transmissions, causing both to transmit simultaneously to the same receiver, resulting in collisions at the receiver (not the sender)
A terminal is physically hidden behind a wall, preventing all wireless communication
The hidden terminal problem only affects wired networks, not wireless
A receiver fails to send ACKs, causing senders to believe the terminal is unreachable
questions.explanation: In wireless, range is limited. Station A transmits to C; station B (out of A's range) also transmits to C. Neither A nor B sense each other, so both send simultaneously, corrupting C's reception. This is invisible to the sender (no collision detected locally). RTS/CTS helps: A sends RTS to C, C replies CTS (which B hears, setting NAV), so B defers. Hidden terminals are a wireless-specific issue.

questions.q: What is the exposed terminal problem in wireless networks?
options: A station defers transmission because it senses activity from a nearby transmitter (outside the receiver's range), unnecessarily reducing channel utilization even though transmission would not cause collision at the actual receiver
A terminal is exposed to interference from unrelated networks on the same frequency
A transmitter sends while receiving, causing self-interference
A receiver is located too far away to detect the sender's transmission
questions.explanation: Exposed terminal is the opposite of hidden terminal: station B senses A transmitting to C and defers, but B could safely transmit to D (out of A-C path) without collision. Sensing overconstrains the network. RTS/CTS also helps here: only the intended receiver (C) replies, so B does not unnecessarily defer. This reduces throughput.

questions.q: What is the RTS/CTS (Request to Send / Clear to Send) mechanism and how does it mitigate hidden terminal problems?
options: A sender transmits RTS (short frame) to reserve the medium; the receiver replies with CTS; other stations hearing RTS or CTS defer transmission until the main transmission completes, reducing collisions
RTS/CTS is a handshake for establishing a secure wireless connection before data transmission
RTS/CTS doubles the overhead but does not improve performance
RTS/CTS only works in cellular networks, not in Wi-Fi
questions.explanation: RTS/CTS is a collision avoidance mechanism in 802.11. Before sending data, the sender transmits RTS (includes duration). The receiver responds with CTS. Stations hearing either frame set NAV (Network Allocation Vector) timers, deferring transmission. This reduces the window during which hidden terminals can collide. Overhead exists but improves performance when frames are long.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the MAC address header in 802.11 wireless frames and how many addresses does it support?
options: The 802.11 MAC header contains four address fields to support sender, receiver, transmitter, and access point, allowing frames to be relayed between networks in infrastructure mode
802.11 MAC headers use only two address fields like Ethernet (source and destination)
The MAC header contains only one address (the transmitter's) for broadcast efficiency
Address fields are 64 bits each, supporting 2^64 unique devices per network
questions.explanation: 802.11 uniquely supports four addresses to handle infrastructure and mesh modes. In infrastructure mode: source station, destination station, AP (transmitter), AP (receiver). This allows frames to traverse multiple hops and network boundaries. Ethernet has two addresses; 802.11 extended this. MAC addresses are 48 bits, not 64.

questions.q: What is the near-far problem in cellular networks and how does it affect wireless communication?
options: A receiver receives strong signals from nearby transmitters and weak signals from distant ones, causing nearby transmitters to dominate and drowning out distant ones; power control and spread spectrum techniques mitigate this
A cellular tower is located too far from users, preventing network coverage
The problem occurs only in wired networks, not cellular
The near-far problem is solved by increasing transmit power for all devices equally
questions.explanation: In wireless, signal strength decreases with distance (path loss). A nearby transmitter can overwhelm a receiver's ability to detect distant transmitters. Power control (dynamic adjustment) lets distant stations increase power while nearby stations reduce it. Spread spectrum (CDMA, WCDMA) allows simultaneous transmissions from different users even with power imbalances. Equal power increases interference.

questions.q: What is fading in wireless channels and what are the main types?
options: Fading is the time-varying attenuation of signals due to multipath propagation; fast fading causes rapid amplitude changes over short distances; slow fading causes gradual changes over large distances; Rayleigh and Rician fading describe statistical models
Fading is the gradual loss of signal as distance increases, the same in all wireless channels
Fading only affects cellular networks, not Wi-Fi
Fading can be completely eliminated by increasing transmit power
questions.explanation: Multipath: signals reflect off buildings, terrain, and objects, arriving at different times and phases. This causes constructive (boost) and destructive (cancel) interference, varying by location and time. Fast fading (changes per meter) is common in mobile systems. Slow fading (changes per hundred meters) is common for distance attenuation. Statistical models (Rayleigh, Rician) describe fading behavior. Power cannot eliminate fading.

questions.q: What is 802.11 channel access and how is interference managed in unlicensed spectrum?
options: 802.11 devices contend for unlicensed spectrum (2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, 6 GHz) using CSMA/CA; channels are defined to reduce overlap; devices sense and defer to avoid collisions with peers; coexistence with other technologies (Bluetooth, microwave ovens) requires frequency agility
Channels are allocated dynamically by a central controller, ensuring no two devices use the same frequency
802.11 uses licensed spectrum exclusively, avoiding interference from other devices
All 802.11 devices transmit on all channels simultaneously for redundancy
questions.explanation: 802.11 operates in unlicensed ISM bands. Multiple channels (11 in 2.4 GHz, 13 in 5 GHz USA) reduce overlap; devices contend within their channel using CSMA/CA. Coexistence with non-802.11 devices (Bluetooth hops across 2.4 GHz; microwave ovens emit noise) causes interference. Techniques: frequency agility, power reduction, better antennas. No central allocator; devices self-organize.

questions.q: What is the difference between ad-hoc and infrastructure modes in 802.11 wireless networks?
options: Infrastructure mode requires an access point (AP) that relays frames between clients; ad-hoc mode has no AP, and stations communicate peer-to-peer directly or via multi-hop relaying
Ad-hoc networks are inherently more secure than infrastructure networks
Both modes require a central controller for authentication
Infrastructure mode is for wireless, ad-hoc mode is for wired networks
questions.explanation: Infrastructure: clients associate with an AP, which bridges to the wired network. Frames from client A to client B go through the AP (four-address frames). Ad-hoc (IBSS): no AP; stations send directly to each other or relay multi-hop. Security is not inherent to mode choice (both can be secure). Controllers are separate from these modes.

questions.q: What is signal-to-interference-plus-noise-ratio (SINR) and why is it critical in wireless network design?
options: SINR (dB) = Power_signal / (Power_interference + Power_noise); higher SINR enables higher data rates via better modulation (QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM); SINR degradation forces step-down to lower rate or loss of connectivity
SINR is identical to SNR and represents only noise, not interference
SINR increases linearly with transmit power, solving wireless capacity problems
SINR is only relevant in cellular networks, not Wi-Fi
questions.explanation: SINR (not SNR, which ignores interference) determines link quality. Higher SINR allows higher-order modulation (more bits per symbol, higher data rate). Interference from other transmitters reduces SINR more than noise alone would. In CDMA cellular systems, power control and interference management are essential. Wi-Fi, LTE, and 5G all rely on SINR; weak SINR forces fallback to 11g (6 Mbps) from 11ac (high Gbps).
